directions.name: Human idea 7 sparse-error repair: keep the half-panel branch, but fix the remaining nondeterministic ownership/overlap bug end to end
directions.hypothesis: Round 2 preserved the only real register-wall breakthrough in the repo: the true hot-band kernel dropped from about 41.03 us to 36.94 us, registers/thread fell from 167 to 93, occupancy_limit_registers rose from 1 to 2, and active warps / tensor active both jumped into the low-30s / low-40s. That is too much real signal to abandon with seven rounds still available. The blocker is correctness. The key new clue is that a local rerun of the same correctness case after round 2 moved the max-error index and value, while the mean_abs_err stayed near 0.033. That is not the signature of one fixed deterministic address formula; it points to a sparse nondeterministic ownership or overlapping-writer bug somewhere in the half-panel control/export contract. The next move should therefore keep the half-panel family but tighten ownership all the way through pass control, compact B staging, and global export so each output tile has one compile-time writer and one compile-time column identity.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Correctness-blocking nondeterministic ownership/coverage bug in the half-panel control/export path, not DRAM bandwidth or the macro tile choice. Success means the same correctness case stops drifting across reruns while preserving the 93-reg / 2-block occupancy signal.
directions.risk: Another repair round can still end with an incorrect kernel if the overlap is deeper than the current ownership helpers imply. The guardrail is to avoid silently re-expanding the live set or undoing the 2-block occupancy gain while chasing correctness.
metrics_to_recheck: same-case rerun stability of max_abs_index / max_abs_output
directions.stop_condition: Stop if repeated correctness reruns still drift after a full ownership/export cleanup, or if fixing correctness forces registers back toward the old 167/thread regime.

directions.name: Human idea 8/9 Pg2s + Ps2r: once correctness is stable, fuse the two 32-column passes into one K-loop so each staged A tile is consumed twice
directions.hypothesis: The half-panel branch broke the register wall, but it still replays the whole producer pipeline twice: each of the two 32-column passes restages the same A tile and pays the same cp.async commit/wait and CTA barrier pattern. Once correctness is stable, the highest-upside follow-up is to keep the 64x32 live set but consume both half panels inside one steady-state K loop so A is staged once and reused across both panels before the pipeline advances. That is the cleanest mapping from the user's `Pg2s`, `Ps2r`, and `Stage` ideas to the remaining performance tax on this branch.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Replay of the A-side producer path and repeated barrier tax across two serial half-panel sweeps. Successful fusion should cut barrier pressure and compute-memory throughput without giving back the current 93-reg occupancy win.
directions.risk: This should not be attempted before correctness is stable. It also risks re-expanding the live fragment set or reintroducing stage-ordering bugs while trying to save A-feed cost.
directions.stop_condition: Only continue if dir_01 makes the branch correct or at least stable across reruns. Reject if registers climb materially above 100 or correctness regresses again.

directions.name: Human idea 5 Bank conflict fallback: restore the accepted 64x384 base and spend the next round on a warp-local B-consumer transform
directions.hypothesis: If the half-panel branch still fails after one more correctness-focused round, the disciplined fallback is not another blind repair; it is to return to the last correct accepted base and attack the original feed/orchestration wall at the warp consumer boundary only. The user explicitly constrained this family: no extra CTA repack, no extra shared tile, no reduced stage count. That still leaves a valid fallback path on the correct 64x384 branch while preserving the measured lesson that the current stable kernel is not DRAM-bound.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared/L1/B-operand delivery behavior on the accepted 64x384 path rather than occupancy. This is lower ceiling than the half-panel branch, but also much lower correctness risk.
directions.risk: This gives up the only path that actually broke through the register wall. It should be used only if one more correctness-repair round fails to stabilize the half-panel branch.
directions.stop_condition: Use only if dir_01 still leaves correctness unstable. Reject any fallback implementation that increases shared memory, adds CTA repack, or reduces stage depth.

notes: Round 3 human-idea audit against the latest measured evidence: `Tiling` stays accepted as the outer family because the 256x128 hot-band branch is the only path that materially broke the register wall; `Coalescing Access`, `Data Reuse`, and `Async Copy` remain accepted as baseline infrastructure because the kernel is already on 16-byte cp.async staging and the profile is still not DRAM-limited; `Bank Conflict` is deferred to the fallback family only, because the current blocker is correctness, not shared-feed throughput on a correct branch; `L2 Cache` stays deferred because neither the latest run nor the accepted base is L2-bound; `Register Reuse` remains the primary accepted family because the half-panel branch is still the only path that reached 93 regs/thread and 2-block occupancy; `Pg2s`, `Ps2r`, and `Stage` are accepted as the next family after correctness because replaying the A producer path across two full half-panel passes is now the clearest remaining tax once the branch is stable. The key new diagnosis signal is local rerun instability: after round 2, rerunning the same correctness case changed the max-error index and value while mean_abs_err stayed near 0.033. That points to a sparse nondeterministic ownership or overlapping-writer bug, not just one fixed deterministic column formula. Recommended direction `dir_01` therefore keeps the high-ceiling half-panel family alive for one more round, but narrows the task to end-to-end ownership stabilization. If that still fails, the next round should pivot to the correct 64x384 fallback.
